audienceModes.label: Mixed
audienceModes.description: A realistic blend of curious and skeptical reactions, roughly balanced.

audienceModes.label: Curious
audienceModes.description: Engaged, asks clarifying follow-up questions, generally receptive.

audienceModes.label: Tough
audienceModes.description: Skeptical of cooperative structures, pushes back on vague claims, interrupts.

modules.title: Welcome to the Collective
modules.summary: Orientation for developers joining the cooperative: what membership means and what changes on day one.
talkingPoints: The Collective is member-owned: developers who join hold a stake in how it runs, not just a listing on a platform.
Every member gets one voice in governance votes, regardless of tenure or project volume.
Membership includes access to shared resources: compute credits, dataset libraries, and a peer mentorship directory.
New members complete a short onboarding review before their profile is visible for project matching.

modules.title: How the Mutual Support System Works
modules.summary: Explains the case-based support model members use when something goes wrong on an engagement.
talkingPoints: Any member can open a case: payment disputes, contract disagreements, or a client complaint.
Cases start at a routine tier and can escalate to a peer mediator, then to the cooperative board if unresolved.
Mediators are elected members, not outside staff, and rotate on a regular schedule.
The goal of mediation is a documented, fair resolution, not punishment of either party.

modules.title: How to Request Help
modules.summary: Practical walkthrough of filing a case or asking for mentorship.
talkingPoints: Requests for help go through the same intake as formal cases, just tagged at a lighter priority.
A peer mentorship request should describe the specific skill gap, not just 'I need help with everything.'
Response times vary by tier: routine requests are best-effort, escalated cases have a due date and named mediator.
Members are encouraged to document what they already tried before asking, so mentors can jump straight to the gap.

modules.title: How to Contribute Resources
modules.summary: Covers the resource-sharing and contribution proposal process.
talkingPoints: Members can propose contributing tools, templates, datasets, or compute time back to the shared pool.
Contribution proposals are reviewed lightly, mostly to confirm licensing is clean and there's no client-confidential material.
Contributing does not create an ownership claim beyond standard cooperative attribution norms.
Frequent contributors are recognized in the Collective's internal directory, which factors into peer trust for project matching.

modules.title: Cooperative Values and Governance Basics
modules.summary: The values underpinning decisions and how governance votes actually happen.
talkingPoints: Core values: shared ownership, transparent dispute resolution, and no member profiting from another member's mediation.
Board seats are filled by member election on a fixed term, with staggered rotation so the whole board never turns over at once.
Major policy changes go to a full membership vote; day-to-day mediation decisions stay with rotating mediators.
This is explicitly not a commission-based marketplace — the Collective does not take a cut of individual client engagements.

qaBank.question: Is this just another freelance marketplace with extra steps?
qaBank.suggestedAnswer: No — there's no commission on client work. Membership is about shared ownership and mutual support, not the platform taking a cut of every project.

qaBank.question: What happens if my case doesn't get resolved at the peer mediator level?
qaBank.suggestedAnswer: It escalates to the cooperative board, which is the final internal tier. Board decisions are documented and shared with both members involved.

qaBank.question: Can I request help anonymously?
qaBank.suggestedAnswer: Case intake requires your member identity so mediators can follow up, but sensitive details can be marked confidential in the case notes.

qaBank.question: Do I get paid for contributing a resource to the shared pool?
qaBank.suggestedAnswer: Not directly — contribution is recognized reputationally and factors into peer trust, not a cash payout.

qaBank.question: Who actually makes the rules here?
qaBank.suggestedAnswer: Members do, through elected board seats and full-membership votes on major policy changes.
